stem: A 32-year-old G1P0 woman at 33 weeks and 2 days gestation attends day assessment with a two-day history of new frontal headache and intermittent right upper quadrant discomfort that has worsened over the last 24 hours. She denies visual disturbance, scotomata, abdominal pain in other regions, vaginal bleeding or reduced fetal movements. Her booking blood pressure at 12 weeks was 108/64 mmHg and all booking bloods (including renal, hepatic and platelet counts) were normal. Her dating ultrasound was reassuring and there is no personal or family history of hypertensive disease. On three repeated measurements 15 minutes apart today her blood pressure is 152/98 mmHg, 154/96 mmHg and 150/99 mmHg in the seated position with an appropriately sized cuff. A spot urinary protein/creatinine ratio is 18 mg/mmol, below the SOMANZ significant proteinuria threshold of 30 mg/mmol, and she has only trace blood on dipstick. The midwife notes hyperreflexia without clonus and asks the obstetric registrar whether the patient meets the diagnostic criteria for pre-eclampsia in the absence of significant proteinuria, given the bedside biochemistry shown below.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 152/98 mmHg (confirmed on three readings), HR 84 bpm, T 36.8 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Hyperreflexia bilaterally without clonus. Mild RUQ tenderness. Uterus soft, fundus appropriate for dates.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 118 g/L, platelets 92 x10^9/L, creatinine 82 micromol/L, ALT 78 U/L (ULN 35), AST 65 U/L (ULN 35), urate 0.38 mmol/L, urine PCR 18 mg/mmol. CTG normal.

lead_in: Which feature in this presentation independently satisfies the SOMANZ diagnostic criteria for pre-eclampsia in the absence of significant proteinuria?

options.text: Serum urate 0.38 mmol/L above the trimester-specific reference range
options.rationale: Hyperuricaemia is associated with pre-eclampsia and useful for prognosis but is not part of the SOMANZ diagnostic criteria for the disorder (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Hyperreflexia on examination without clonus
options.rationale: Hyperreflexia is a clinical sign that raises suspicion of central nervous system involvement but is not a SOMANZ diagnostic criterion; eclampsia, persistent severe headache, visual scotomata or stroke are the neurological criteria (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Platelet count 92 x10^9/L with ALT and AST more than twice the upper limit of normal
options.rationale: SOMANZ defines pre-eclampsia as new hypertension after 20 weeks with at least one maternal end-organ feature or uteroplacental dysfunction. Thrombocytopenia below 100 x10^9/L and transaminases more than twice the ULN are each independently diagnostic criteria (SOMANZ 2014; RANZCOG 2023).

options.text: New right upper quadrant pain in isolation
options.rationale: Epigastric or RUQ pain raises suspicion for hepatic capsular stretch but is not a stand-alone SOMANZ diagnostic criterion without biochemical confirmation of hepatic dysfunction (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Urine protein/creatinine ratio of 18 mg/mmol
options.rationale: Significant proteinuria by SOMANZ requires a PCR of 30 mg/mmol or greater, or a 24-hour urine protein of 300 mg or more; 18 mg/mmol is below threshold (SOMANZ 2014).

explanation.summary: Pre-eclampsia is diagnosed when new hypertension after 20 weeks is accompanied by either significant proteinuria OR any maternal end-organ dysfunction (renal, hepatic, haematological, neurological, pulmonary oedema) OR uteroplacental dysfunction. Proteinuria is no longer mandatory.
key_points: SOMANZ end-organ criteria: platelets <100 x10^9/L, creatinine >90 micromol/L, ALT/AST >2x ULN, pulmonary oedema, eclampsia/severe headache/visual symptoms/stroke
Uteroplacental dysfunction: fetal growth restriction, abnormal Dopplers, stillbirth
Significant proteinuria: PCR >=30 mg/mmol or 24-hour >=300 mg
Hyperuricaemia is associated but not a diagnostic criterion
explanation.pearls: Always check platelets, creatinine and transaminases in any pregnant woman with new hypertension after 20 weeks; pre-eclampsia can present without proteinuria.

sources.label: SOMANZ Guideline for the management of hypertensive disorders of pregnancy

stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 woman at 35 weeks and 1 day gestation is admitted to the birth suite with severe pre-eclampsia. Her blood pressure is 168/112 mmHg confirmed on three measurements 15 minutes apart despite oral labetalol 200 mg taken two hours ago, she reports a brisk frontal headache and visual scotomata, platelets are 86 x10^9/L and ALT is 130 U/L (more than three times the upper limit of normal). There is no clonus and no focal neurology; reflexes are 3+. Renal function is normal with creatinine 68 micromol/L and urine output is 60 mL/h over the last four hours. CTG shows a reactive trace with no decelerations and the fetus is appropriately grown on recent ultrasound. The consultant decides to commence intravenous magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis prior to expedited delivery, and asks the registrar to set up the SOMANZ/RANZCOG infusion regimen. The midwife asks specifically about the correct loading dose, the maintenance infusion rate, the monitoring required during the infusion, and what to do if a generalised tonic-clonic seizure occurs while the patient is already on the maintenance infusion.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 168/112 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, RR 16 /min, SpO2 98%.
data_table.Examination: Brisk reflexes 3+, no clonus. Chest clear. Uterus soft.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 112 g/L, platelets 86 x10^9/L, creatinine 68 micromol/L, ALT 130 U/L, AST 105 U/L, urate 0.42 mmol/L.

lead_in: Which magnesium sulfate regimen most closely matches Australian (SOMANZ/RANZCOG) recommendations for this patient?

options.text: Load 2 g IV over 20 minutes, then 0.5 g/h maintenance, and 1 g IV bolus if a seizure occurs
options.rationale: The load (2 g) and maintenance rate (0.5 g/h) are both subtherapeutic compared with the SOMANZ regimen (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Load 4 g IV over 15-20 minutes, then 1 g/h maintenance, with hourly monitoring of reflexes, respiratory rate and urine output; recurrent seizures treated with a further 2-4 g IV bolus
options.rationale: This matches the SOMANZ and RANZCOG regimen: 4 g IV loading dose over 15-20 minutes, 1 g/h maintenance infusion, hourly reflexes/RR/UO monitoring, and 2-4 g IV bolus for breakthrough seizures (SOMANZ 2014; RANZCOG 2023).

options.text: Load 6 g IV over 20 minutes, then 2 g/h maintenance, with no monitoring required while urine output is adequate
options.rationale: The maintenance rate is double the recommended dose and reflex/respiratory monitoring is mandatory due to the narrow therapeutic window of magnesium (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Load 10 g IM (5 g into each buttock) without an IV loading dose, then 5 g IM every 4 hours
options.rationale: The IM-only Pritchard regimen is used in low-resource settings; Australian practice uses the IV regimen which is more controllable and less painful (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Load 4 g IV over 5 minutes, then 1 g/h maintenance, ceasing the infusion if patellar reflexes are absent and giving calcium chloride only if respiratory rate falls below 12
options.rationale: The loading dose should be given over 15-20 minutes, not 5 minutes, to avoid hypotension and bradycardia. Absent reflexes signal toxicity and warrant calcium gluconate 1 g IV (SOMANZ 2014).

explanation.summary: SOMANZ/RANZCOG magnesium sulfate regimen for severe pre-eclampsia and eclampsia: 4 g IV loading over 15-20 minutes, then 1 g/h maintenance. Monitor reflexes, RR and UO hourly. Treat recurrent seizures with a further 2-4 g IV bolus. Calcium gluconate 1 g IV is the antidote for toxicity.
key_points: Loading dose: 4 g IV over 15-20 min in 100 mL saline
Maintenance: 1 g/h IV (reduce if renal impairment)
Recurrent seizure: further 2-4 g IV bolus over 5 minutes
Toxicity signs: loss of reflexes, RR <12, UO <100 mL/4 h
Antidote: calcium gluconate 1 g (10 mL of 10%) IV slow
explanation.pearls: Magnesium reduces eclampsia by half compared with placebo and is more effective than phenytoin or diazepam (Magpie trial; Cochrane).

stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 woman at 37 weeks and 4 days gestation, previously well with an uncomplicated pregnancy until last week when her blood pressure was first noted to be 142/88 mmHg, is brought to the birth suite by her partner after a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure at home lasting two minutes followed by a period of unresponsiveness. She has had a worsening frontal headache, blurred vision and epigastric pain for 24 hours. On arrival she is post-ictal but rousable to voice. Her blood pressure is 175/115 mmHg and 172/110 mmHg on repeat 5 minutes apart with an appropriately sized cuff. She has 3+ proteinuria on dipstick, mild peripheral oedema and hyperreflexia with three beats of clonus. There is no focal neurology, no neck stiffness and no fever, making other intracranial causes less likely. Fetal heart rate on CTG is 145 bpm with reduced variability and no decelerations. The senior registrar is in the room, the anaesthetist and obstetric consultant are being paged, an IV line is in situ and bloods are being drawn for urgent FBC, coagulation, U&E and LFTs.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 175/115 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, RR 18 /min, SpO2 96% on 4 L O2, GCS 13 (E3V4M6).
data_table.Examination: Post-ictal, drowsy but rousable. Reflexes 4+ with 3 beats clonus. Tongue laterally bitten.
data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick: protein 3+, blood trace. Bedside Hb 116 g/L, platelets pending. CTG: baseline 145, reduced variability, no decelerations.

lead_in: Which sequence of immediate management steps is most appropriate?

options.text: Immediate caesarean section under general anaesthesia before commencing magnesium sulfate
options.rationale: Maternal stabilisation with magnesium and blood pressure control must precede delivery; rushing to theatre without stabilisation worsens maternal and fetal outcomes (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: IV diazepam 10 mg as first-line anticonvulsant, then phenytoin loading dose, then deliver
options.rationale: Magnesium sulfate is superior to both diazepam and phenytoin for eclampsia (Magpie trial; Cochrane) and is the first-line drug. Benzodiazepines are reserved for refractory seizures (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Sublingual nifedipine to rapidly reduce blood pressure, then transfer to theatre
options.rationale: Sublingual nifedipine causes precipitous, uncontrolled drops in blood pressure with risk of placental hypoperfusion and is not recommended; oral nifedipine SR or IV labetalol/hydralazine is preferred (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Place the patient in the left lateral position, secure airway and give oxygen, load with magnesium sulfate 4 g IV over 15-20 minutes followed by 1 g/h maintenance, control BP with IV labetalol or hydralazine, then expedite delivery once stabilised
options.rationale: This is the standard SOMANZ/RANZCOG sequence: left lateral position to reduce aortocaval compression and aspiration risk, airway/oxygen, magnesium loading and maintenance, antihypertensive control aiming for systolic <160 and diastolic <110, then expedited delivery (vaginal or caesarean) once mother is stable (SOMANZ 2014; RANZCOG 2023).

options.text: Continuous EEG monitoring before commencing any anticonvulsant
options.rationale: EEG is not required to diagnose or treat eclampsia in this clinical setting; treatment is initiated on clinical grounds (SOMANZ 2014).

explanation.summary: Eclampsia management priorities (mother first, then fetus): left lateral, ABC, magnesium sulfate load and maintenance, control severe hypertension, then expedite delivery. Magnesium is first-line, not benzodiazepines.
key_points: Mother before fetus: stabilise then deliver
Magnesium 4 g IV load, 1 g/h maintenance
BP targets: SBP <160, DBP <110 with IV labetalol/hydralazine or oral nifedipine SR
Continue magnesium for 24 h after delivery or last seizure
Avoid sublingual nifedipine and diazepam first-line
explanation.pearls: Up to one third of eclamptic seizures occur postpartum, most within 48 hours; magnesium continues for 24 h post-delivery.

subtopic: HELLP vs AFLP vs TTP vs HUS in pregnancy
stem: A 31-year-old G3P2 woman at 35 weeks and 2 days gestation presents with three days of progressively worsening right upper quadrant pain, nausea, and increasing fatigue. She has not had a seizure but has a mild bifrontal headache and no visual scotomata. Her two previous pregnancies were uncomplicated and she has no personal or family history of thrombotic microangiopathy, autoimmune disease or liver disease. Her blood pressure on three readings is 146-150 / 90-94 mmHg, she has 1+ proteinuria with a urine PCR of 42 mg/mmol, and the fetal CTG is reactive with normal growth on a scan one week ago. She has had no recent gastroenteritis, no bloody diarrhoea and no recent E. coli O157:H7 or shigatoxin-producing organism exposure. Bedside bloods show haemoglobin 96 g/L (anaemia with reticulocytosis), platelets 68 x10^9/L, ALT 285 U/L, AST 312 U/L, total bilirubin 38 micromol/L (predominantly indirect), LDH 850 U/L, fibrinogen 3.2 g/L (preserved), INR 1.1, creatinine 84 micromol/L (normal for pregnancy), glucose 5.2 mmol/L, and ammonia within the normal range. A peripheral blood film shows schistocytes, polychromasia and absent platelet clumps. ADAMTS13 activity measured urgently is 65% (well above the 10% threshold for TTP).

data_table.Vital signs: BP 148/92 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, T 36.9 degC.
data_table.Examination: Mild scleral icterus, RUQ tenderness. Uterus appropriate for dates, soft.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 96 g/L, platelets 68 x10^9/L, schistocytes on film, LDH 850 U/L, bilirubin 38 micromol/L (mostly indirect), ALT 285 U/L, AST 312 U/L, INR 1.1, fibrinogen 3.2 g/L, creatinine 84 micromol/L, glucose 5.2 mmol/L, ammonia normal, ADAMTS13 65%.

lead_in: Which diagnosis best fits this clinical and biochemical picture?

options.text: HELLP syndrome
options.rationale: HELLP is diagnosed by Haemolysis (anaemia, schistocytes, raised LDH, raised indirect bilirubin), Elevated Liver enzymes (ALT/AST >70 U/L typically) and Low Platelets (<100 x10^9/L), usually with normal coagulation and normal glucose. This patient has all three features with preserved fibrinogen, normal INR, normal glucose and ADAMTS13 >10%, distinguishing it from AFLP, TTP and HUS (RANZCOG 2023; SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy
options.rationale: AFLP typically causes hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy (raised INR, low fibrinogen), hyperammonaemia and more pronounced hyperbilirubinaemia. This patient has normal glucose, normal INR and fibrinogen and normal ammonia (RCOG; BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura
options.rationale: TTP is defined by ADAMTS13 activity <10%, with severe thrombocytopenia, microangiopathic haemolysis, neurological features and fever; ADAMTS13 of 65% effectively excludes TTP (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Atypical haemolytic uraemic syndrome
options.rationale: HUS is dominated by acute kidney injury and microangiopathic haemolysis with relatively preserved transaminases; this patient has normal creatinine and very high transaminases, making HUS unlikely (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy
options.rationale: ICP causes pruritus and isolated raised bile acids and modest transaminitis without thrombocytopenia or haemolysis (RANZCOG ICP statement).

explanation.summary: HELLP, AFLP, TTP and HUS can all present with thrombocytopenia and microangiopathic features in late pregnancy. Discriminators: HELLP has high LFTs with normal coags and glucose; AFLP has hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy and hyperammonaemia; TTP has ADAMTS13 <10%; HUS is renal-predominant.
key_points: HELLP: Hb low, platelets <100, AST/ALT >70, LDH >600, INR normal, glucose normal
AFLP: hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy, hyperammonaemia, AKI
TTP: ADAMTS13 <10%, severe thrombocytopenia, neuro features
HUS: AKI dominant, often post-diarrhoeal (STEC) or complement-mediated
Mississippi classification grades HELLP severity by platelet count
explanation.pearls: When in doubt, send fibrinogen, glucose, ammonia and ADAMTS13 - they discriminate HELLP from its mimics within hours.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice - HELLP, AFLP, TTP, HUS

subtopic: Gestational diabetes - ADIPS 75 g OGTT thresholds
stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 woman attends for her routine 26-week screening visit. Her booking BMI was 27, she has no family history of diabetes, no previous GDM and no PCOS. She undergoes a 75 g oral glucose tolerance test as part of universal screening at 24-28 weeks gestation in line with Australian (ADIPS) recommendations. The midwife receives the laboratory result and asks the registrar to confirm the venous plasma glucose thresholds that define gestational diabetes mellitus on a 75 g OGTT in Australia. Her result will need to be compared against the ADIPS 2014 diagnostic cut-offs at fasting, 1 hour and 2 hours.
lead_in: Which set of 75 g OGTT venous plasma glucose thresholds defines gestational diabetes in Australia (ADIPS)?

options.text: Fasting >=5.5, 1 h >=10.0, 2 h >=8.5 mmol/L
options.rationale: The fasting threshold is 5.1 mmol/L, not 5.5; 5.5 was the older Australasian Diabetes in Pregnancy Society pre-2014 threshold (ADIPS 2014).

options.text: Fasting >=5.1, 1 h >=10.0, 2 h >=8.5 mmol/L
options.rationale: The ADIPS 2014 (HAPO-derived, IADPSG-aligned) diagnostic thresholds for GDM on a 75 g OGTT are fasting >=5.1, 1-hour >=10.0 and 2-hour >=8.5 mmol/L; one or more abnormal values is sufficient for diagnosis (ADIPS 2014; RANZCOG 2023).

options.text: Fasting >=5.1, 1 h >=10.0, 2 h >=7.8 mmol/L
options.rationale: 2-hour >=7.8 mmol/L is the WHO non-pregnant impaired glucose tolerance threshold, not the GDM threshold (ADIPS 2014; WHO).
These are the diagnostic thresholds for diabetes mellitus outside pregnancy (WHO), much higher than the GDM cut-offs (ADIPS 2014).

options.text: Fasting >=4.5, 1 h >=9.0, 2 h >=7.5 mmol/L
options.rationale: These values are below the ADIPS thresholds and are not used in any major guideline (ADIPS 2014).

explanation.summary: Australia uses ADIPS 2014 (IADPSG-aligned) criteria: 75 g OGTT at 24-28 weeks for universal screening. Any one value at or above fasting 5.1, 1 h 10.0 or 2 h 8.5 mmol/L makes the diagnosis.
key_points: Universal OGTT at 24-28 weeks
Early OGTT in first or early second trimester if high risk (previous GDM, BMI >=30, age >=40, ethnicity, family hx, PCOS, prior macrosomia)
One abnormal value is enough
Repeat 75 g OGTT 6-12 weeks postpartum
explanation.pearls: Memorise 5.1 / 10.0 / 8.5 - they are the only three numbers you need for diagnosis in Australia.

subtopic: GDM - insulin titration after lifestyle failure
stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 woman at 30 weeks and 2 days gestation was diagnosed with GDM at 26 weeks (fasting 5.4 mmol/L, 2 h 9.1 mmol/L on 75 g OGTT). She has been on structured dietary modification with a credentialled dietitian and 30 minutes of daily walking for four weeks. Her four-times-daily capillary glucose diary now shows fasting values consistently 5.6-6.2 mmol/L (ADIPS target <5.1 mmol/L) and 2-hour postprandial values 5.8-6.8 mmol/L (target <6.7 mmol/L) after all three meals. There is no overnight hypoglycaemia, no symptomatic hypoglycaemia, no glycosuria and no ketonuria on bedside testing. Fetal ultrasound at 30 weeks shows estimated fetal weight on the 78th centile with appropriate liquor and normal umbilical and middle cerebral artery Dopplers. She has no contraindication to insulin or metformin, her renal function is normal (creatinine 58 micromol/L), and she has good health literacy and dexterity for self-injection. Her booking BMI was 31 and she has gained 6 kg in pregnancy.

data_table.Investigations: BSL diary: fasting 5.6-6.2; postprandial within target after all meals. HbA1c 5.6%. Renal function normal.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Commence basal-bolus insulin with rapid-acting analogue with each meal and isophane (NPH) at bedtime
options.rationale: Full basal-bolus is reserved for patients with both elevated fasting and postprandial values. This patient has isolated elevated fasting glucose only, so basal insulin alone is sufficient (ADIPS 2014; RANZCOG 2023).

options.text: Commence rapid-acting insulin (aspart or lispro) before each meal only
options.rationale: Prandial insulin would not address the predominant fasting hyperglycaemia and would risk daytime hypoglycaemia (ADIPS 2014).

options.text: Continue lifestyle only and review in four weeks
options.rationale: Fasting glucose remains above target despite four weeks of optimised lifestyle; pharmacotherapy is indicated to reduce risk of macrosomia and stillbirth (ADIPS 2014).

options.text: Commence empagliflozin 10 mg daily
options.rationale: SGLT2 inhibitors are contraindicated in pregnancy due to teratogenicity concerns and lack of safety data (TGA; AMH).

options.text: Commence isophane (NPH) insulin at bedtime, titrating to fasting capillary glucose target
options.rationale: For isolated fasting hyperglycaemia in GDM, the ADIPS-recommended first-line pharmacotherapy is bedtime intermediate-acting (isophane/NPH) insulin titrated to achieve fasting glucose <5.1 mmol/L. Metformin is an acceptable alternative or adjunct in suitable patients but insulin is the established first-line agent (ADIPS 2014; RANZCOG 2023).

explanation.summary: When lifestyle fails in GDM, pharmacotherapy is matched to the pattern of hyperglycaemia: isolated fasting hyperglycaemia -> bedtime isophane (NPH); isolated postprandial -> prandial rapid-acting insulin; both -> basal-bolus regimen. Metformin is an option after counselling about placental transfer.
key_points: ADIPS targets: fasting <5.1, 1 h <7.4, 2 h <6.7 mmol/L
Isolated fasting hyperglycaemia: bedtime NPH first
Isolated postprandial: prandial rapid-acting analogue
Both: basal-bolus
Metformin: alternative or adjunct; crosses placenta; not for severe hyperglycaemia
SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 agonists: contraindicated in pregnancy
explanation.pearls: Match the insulin to the glucose pattern. Don't reach for basal-bolus when only the fasting value is high.

stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 woman at 28 weeks gestation has had three antenatal visits over a fortnight with persistently elevated blood pressure readings (142/92, 146/94 and today 144/90 mmHg on repeated measurement with an appropriately sized cuff after five minutes of rest). There is no proteinuria on dipstick, urinary PCR is 12 mg/mmol, full blood count, electrolytes, urate and transaminases are within normal limits, and her fetal growth is on the 50th centile with normal umbilical artery and middle cerebral artery Dopplers. She is asymptomatic with no headache, no visual disturbance and no abdominal pain. She has no history of asthma, no heart block, no significant bradycardia, no liver disease, no known drug allergies and is not taking any over-the-counter medications. She is also currently breastfeeding her partner's older child as a tandem feed (she is a wet-nurse for a friend), so the consultant reminds the registrar that the choice of first-line oral antihypertensive should be both safe in pregnancy and compatible with lactation.

data_table.Examination: Uncomplicated. No oedema. Fundus appropriate for dates.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 122 g/L, platelets 240 x10^9/L, creatinine 62 micromol/L, ALT 22 U/L, urine PCR <30 mg/mmol.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line oral antihypertensive in this Australian setting?

options.text: Labetalol 100 mg orally twice daily, titrated to BP target
options.rationale: Labetalol is the SOMANZ/RANZCOG recommended first-line oral antihypertensive in pregnancy. It is safe in lactation and has the strongest evidence base. Starting dose 100 mg BD, up to 400 mg TDS (SOMANZ 2014; RANZCOG 2023).

options.text: Methyldopa 250 mg orally three times daily
options.rationale: Methyldopa is a second-line agent in current Australian practice due to slower onset, sedation and mood effects, and the risk of postpartum depression with continued use; labetalol is preferred first-line (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Nifedipine slow-release 20 mg orally twice daily
options.rationale: Nifedipine SR is an effective second-line oral agent in pregnancy. While reasonable, labetalol remains first-line per SOMANZ unless contraindicated (e.g. asthma, heart block) (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Hydralazine 25 mg orally three times daily
options.rationale: Oral hydralazine is rarely used as monotherapy due to reflex tachycardia and lupus-like syndrome; IV hydralazine has a role in acute severe hypertension (SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Enalapril 5 mg orally daily
options.rationale: ACE inhibitors are contraindicated in the second and third trimesters due to fetal renal damage, oligohydramnios and skull hypoplasia (TGA; AMH).

explanation.summary: First-line oral antihypertensives for non-severe hypertension in pregnancy in Australia: labetalol > nifedipine SR > methyldopa. ACE inhibitors and ARBs are contraindicated. Treatment target BP 110-140 / 80-90 mmHg per SOMANZ.
key_points: Labetalol 100-400 mg BD-TDS (first-line)
Nifedipine SR 20-60 mg BD (second-line)
Methyldopa 250-500 mg TDS-QID (second/third-line)
ACE inhibitors and ARBs: contraindicated
Acute severe BP: IV labetalol or IV hydralazine or oral nifedipine SR
explanation.pearls: Avoid labetalol if the patient has asthma, heart block, or significant bradycardia; switch to nifedipine SR.

stem: A 34-year-old G3P2 woman at 12 weeks and 1 day gestation has a booking weight of 98 kg with a booking BMI of 35 kg/m2. She had a confirmed left-sided proximal deep vein thrombosis in the puerperium of her last pregnancy three years ago, two weeks after a normal vaginal birth, treated with six months of therapeutic enoxaparin. She is not on long-term anticoagulation. She has no other VTE risk factors beyond the previous pregnancy-related event - no thrombophilia (tested negative), no family history of VTE, no smoking, no significant immobility, and no varicose veins. The booking team performs a RANZCOG/RCOG-aligned VTE risk assessment and decides she requires antenatal pharmacological thromboprophylaxis commencing in the first trimester and continuing for six weeks postpartum. She has no renal impairment (creatinine 64 micromol/L, eGFR >90), no active bleeding, no antepartum haemorrhage, no allergy to LMWH, no recent heparin exposure and platelets are 280 x10^9/L. She is reviewed in the obstetric medicine clinic for prescribing of the correct weight-banded dose using the RANZCOG-adopted RCOG Green-top 37a weight bands, which are: 50-90 kg, 91-130 kg, 131-170 kg, and above 170 kg. She has been counselled about self-administration of subcutaneous LMWH and accepts daily injections. The obstetric medicine consultant asks the registrar to prescribe the prophylactic dose that corresponds to her booking weight, with a plan to continue throughout pregnancy and for six weeks postpartum.

data_table.Examination: Booking weight 98 kg, BMI 35. No peripheral oedema, calves soft.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 124 g/L, platelets 280 x10^9/L, creatinine 64 micromol/L, eGFR >90, thrombophilia screen negative.

lead_in: Which weight-adjusted enoxaparin dose is most appropriate for antenatal thromboprophylaxis in this patient?

options.text: Enoxaparin 20 mg subcutaneously daily
options.rationale: 20 mg daily is below the thromboprophylaxis dose for any maternal weight band and would be subtherapeutic (RCOG Green-top 37a; RANZCOG).

options.text: Enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily
options.rationale: 40 mg daily is the prophylactic dose for women weighing 50-90 kg. At 98 kg booking weight, the appropriate band is 91-130 kg, which requires 60 mg daily (RCOG Green-top 37a; RANZCOG).

options.text: Enoxaparin 60 mg subcutaneously daily
options.rationale: Weight-banded antenatal prophylaxis (RCOG Green-top 37a, adopted by RANZCOG): 50-90 kg = 40 mg; 91-130 kg = 60 mg; 131-170 kg = 80 mg; >170 kg = 0.6 mg/kg daily. At 98 kg this patient falls into the 91-130 kg band, requiring 60 mg enoxaparin SC daily (RCOG; RANZCOG).

options.text: Enoxaparin 80 mg subcutaneously daily
options.rationale: 80 mg daily is the prophylactic dose for women weighing 131-170 kg; this patient at 98 kg requires 60 mg (RCOG Green-top 37a).

options.text: Enoxaparin 1 mg/kg subcutaneously twice daily (therapeutic dose)
options.rationale: Therapeutic anticoagulation is not indicated for prophylaxis after a single previous provoked (pregnancy-related) VTE; this woman requires prophylactic dosing antenatally and for 6 weeks postpartum (RCOG Green-top 37a).

explanation.summary: Antenatal VTE prophylaxis with enoxaparin uses weight bands (RCOG Green-top 37a, adopted by RANZCOG). Previous VTE generally indicates antenatal prophylaxis from the first trimester and continuation for 6 weeks postpartum.
key_points: 50-90 kg: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily
91-130 kg: 60 mg SC daily
131-170 kg: 80 mg SC daily
>170 kg: 0.6 mg/kg/day
Previous unprovoked or oestrogen-related VTE: prophylaxis from T1, continue 6 weeks postpartum
Previous provoked VTE not related to pregnancy: postpartum prophylaxis only
explanation.pearls: Use booking weight; do not adjust prophylaxis weight bands as pregnancy progresses unless weight gain is extreme.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top Guideline 37a: Thrombosis in pregnancy

stem: A 30-year-old G1P0 woman at 28 weeks and 3 days gestation presents to the obstetric assessment unit with a three-day history of fever to 38.6 degC, generalised myalgia, frontal headache and mild non-bloody diarrhoea. She reports having eaten unpasteurised soft cheese from a delicatessen at a friend's gathering one week earlier and recalls that several other guests have since had similar mild symptoms. There is no rash, no respiratory symptoms, no neck stiffness, no urinary symptoms and no contact with anyone with a confirmed infectious diagnosis. Fetal movements remain normal and CTG is reactive with a baseline of 145 bpm. Blood cultures are taken before any antibiotics are commenced and a urine culture is sent. The microbiology registrar advises that pending blood cultures, empirical therapy should cover the most likely food-borne organism in this setting. She has no penicillin allergy, no other drug allergies, normal renal function and is not taking any other medication beyond pregnancy supplements.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6 degC, BP 110/72 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, RR 18 /min.
data_table.Examination: Looks unwell but not septic. Chest clear. Abdomen soft. Uterus non-tender, fundus appropriate.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 14.5 x10^9/L (neutrophilia), CRP 92 mg/L, blood cultures taken. CTG reactive.

lead_in: Which empirical antimicrobial regimen is most appropriate?

options.text: Ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily monotherapy
options.rationale: Listeria monocytogenes is intrinsically resistant to all cephalosporins; ceftriaxone has no useful activity against Listeria (Therapeutic Guidelines; eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Amoxicillin 1 g orally three times daily
options.rationale: Oral amoxicillin is inadequate for invasive listeriosis with bacteraemia in pregnancy; IV therapy with high-dose ampicillin (or benzylpenicillin) plus gentamicin synergy is required (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole IV
options.rationale: Co-trimoxazole is the preferred agent only for penicillin-allergic patients with listeriosis; the sulfa component is also relatively contraindicated near term due to kernicterus risk (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Ampicillin (or benzylpenicillin) 2 g IV every 6 hours plus gentamicin 4-5 mg/kg IV daily
options.rationale: Suspected listeriosis in pregnancy with fever after high-risk food exposure is treated empirically with high-dose IV ampicillin or benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin for synergy, pending blood cultures (Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic; RANZCOG; CDNA).

options.text: Vancomycin 25 mg/kg IV loading then weight-based maintenance
options.rationale: Vancomycin has variable efficacy against Listeria and is not first-line; ampicillin plus gentamicin is preferred (Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Listeria monocytogenes is a food-borne Gram-positive rod (intracellular) acquired from unpasteurised dairy, soft cheese, deli meats, smoked seafood and pre-prepared salads. Pregnancy increases susceptibility 10-20 fold. Treatment is IV ampicillin (or benzylpenicillin) plus gentamicin.
key_points: Cephalosporins do NOT cover Listeria
Empirical Rx: ampicillin 2 g IV q6h + gentamicin 4-5 mg/kg/day
Duration: 2-3 weeks (longer if meningitis)
Penicillin-allergic: co-trimoxazole
Notify public health (CDNA SoNG)
Fetal complications: stillbirth, preterm labour, neonatal sepsis, granulomatosis infantiseptica
explanation.pearls: Any febrile pregnant woman with a high-risk food history needs blood cultures and listeria-active empirical antibiotics before culture results return.

sources.label: CDNA Series of National Guidelines - Listeriosis

stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 woman at 11 weeks and 2 days gestation has a routine booking infection screen which shows Toxoplasma gondii IgM positive and IgG positive with low avidity, a pattern strongly suggesting recent primary infection within the last 12-16 weeks. She has had a new indoor kitten at home since six weeks before conception and recalls cleaning the litter tray on multiple occasions without gloves. She also recalls eating undercooked lamb skewers at a barbecue 10 weeks ago. She is asymptomatic, has no lymphadenopathy on examination and her partner is well with no symptoms. Toxoplasma PCR on maternal blood is positive. Amniocentesis is being arranged for fetal PCR at 18 weeks (consistent with the recommendation to wait at least six weeks after maternal seroconversion). Her booking ultrasound today shows a viable singleton with normal anatomy for gestation and no calcifications. She is referred to maternal-fetal medicine to discuss treatment options pending fetal status, and asks specifically whether the medication chosen now should change if fetal infection is later confirmed on amniotic fluid PCR. The maternal-fetal medicine consultant also outlines the typical vertical transmission risk by trimester and explains why the timing of investigations matters, before turning to the choice of initial pharmacotherapy.

data_table.Investigations: Toxoplasma IgM positive, IgG positive low avidity, maternal blood PCR positive. Booking ultrasound: viable singleton at 11 weeks, normal anatomy for gestation.

lead_in: Which initial pharmacological strategy is most appropriate?

options.text: Spiramycin 1 g orally three times daily, with a switch to sulfadiazine plus pyrimethamine plus folinic acid only if amniotic fluid PCR confirms fetal infection at 18 weeks
options.rationale: Spiramycin is the first-trimester treatment of choice as it concentrates in the placenta and reduces vertical transmission without causing fetal harm. If fetal infection is confirmed (positive amniotic fluid PCR after 18 weeks), therapy switches to sulfadiazine + pyrimethamine + folinic acid to treat established fetal infection (RANZCOG; BMJ Best Practice; RCOG).

options.text: Sulfadiazine plus pyrimethamine plus folinic acid commenced immediately, regardless of fetal status
options.rationale: Pyrimethamine is a folate antagonist and is teratogenic in the first trimester (neural tube defects, oral clefts); it should be avoided before 14-18 weeks. Treatment of the fetus is reserved for confirmed fetal infection (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Co-trimoxazole monotherapy
options.rationale: Co-trimoxazole has activity against Toxoplasma but is not standard first-line therapy in pregnancy; spiramycin is preferred to prevent transmission (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Azithromycin 500 mg daily
options.rationale: Azithromycin has weak in vitro activity but is not a recommended first-line agent in pregnancy (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Termination of pregnancy without treatment due to certain severe congenital toxoplasmosis
options.rationale: Maternal primary infection in the first trimester carries a relatively low vertical transmission risk (10-15%) although consequences if transmitted can be severe; treatment with spiramycin substantially reduces transmission. Termination is not the default management (RCOG; BMJ Best Practice).

explanation.summary: Suspected maternal toxoplasmosis is confirmed by serology (IgG avidity, IgM) and maternal PCR. Spiramycin reduces transmission without teratogenicity. Confirmed fetal infection (amniotic PCR after 18 weeks) is treated with sulfadiazine + pyrimethamine + folinic acid.
key_points: Spiramycin: first-line in pregnancy to prevent transmission (does not cross placenta well)
Sulfadiazine + pyrimethamine + folinic acid: for confirmed fetal infection from 14-18 weeks
Folinic acid (leucovorin) protects against pyrimethamine-induced bone marrow toxicity
Diagnose primary infection with low IgG avidity within 16-20 weeks of seroconversion
Vertical transmission risk: T1 ~10-15%, T2 ~30%, T3 ~60% - but severity decreases with gestation
Sources: cat faeces, raw/undercooked meat, unwashed produce, soil
explanation.pearls: Earlier infection equals lower transmission but more severe disease if transmitted; later infection equals higher transmission but milder disease.

subtopic: CMV in pregnancy - primary vs non-primary infection
stem: A 29-year-old G2P1 woman at 14 weeks and 1 day gestation works as a long-day-care educator caring for children aged 12-24 months and presents to her GP with a fortnight of mild fatigue, low-grade fever, headache, mild cervical lymphadenopathy and arthralgia. She has no rash, no respiratory symptoms and no jaundice. Routine TORCH testing shows CMV IgM positive, CMV IgG positive with low avidity (consistent with infection within the past three months). Critically, her booking CMV IgG four weeks earlier was negative, providing serological evidence of seroconversion in early pregnancy. A maternal blood CMV PCR is positive at a moderate viral load. She has no prior infectious diseases history, no immunosuppression, and her previous pregnancy was uncomplicated with a healthy term infant. She asks the maternal-fetal medicine consultant about the difference between primary and non-primary (reactivation or reinfection) maternal CMV infection in pregnancy, the next investigation step including fetal surveillance and timing of any amniocentesis, and the role and evidence base for any antiviral therapy in pregnancy.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 degC, BP 110/72 mmHg, HR 84 bpm.
data_table.Examination: Bilateral non-tender cervical lymphadenopathy; otherwise unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: CMV IgG positive low avidity, IgM positive, blood PCR positive. Booking CMV IgG (4 weeks prior): negative. LFTs mildly raised (ALT 65). Hb 122 g/L.

lead_in: Which statement most accurately describes the current Australian approach to maternal CMV infection in this case?

options.text: Non-primary CMV infection carries a higher vertical transmission rate than primary infection, so reassurance is appropriate
options.rationale: Primary maternal CMV has a substantially higher vertical transmission rate (~30-40%) and a higher rate of severe sequelae than non-primary infection (~1-2% transmission, lower severity) (RANZCOG; BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Maternal IV ganciclovir is the standard of care to prevent fetal infection
options.rationale: IV ganciclovir is teratogenic in animal studies and is not routinely used in pregnancy; valaciclovir or valganciclovir trials have shown some benefit but no agent is universally recommended (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Spiramycin reduces vertical transmission of CMV as it does for Toxoplasma
options.rationale: Spiramycin has no role in CMV management; it is specific to toxoplasmosis (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: CMV is a rare cause of congenital infection and routine fetal surveillance is unnecessary after maternal seroconversion
options.rationale: Congenital CMV is the most common congenital infection in developed countries (around 0.5-1% of live births) and the leading non-genetic cause of sensorineural hearing loss; serial fetal ultrasound and consideration of amniotic PCR after 21 weeks are recommended (RANZCOG).

options.text: Confirmed primary maternal CMV infection warrants serial fetal ultrasound for CNS and growth abnormalities, with amniocentesis for CMV PCR offered after 21 weeks gestation and at least 6 weeks after maternal seroconversion; high-dose valaciclovir may be considered to reduce vertical transmission after MFM discussion
options.rationale: This reflects the current RANZCOG/RCOG/BMJ Best Practice approach: confirm primary infection (low avidity, seroconversion), serial fetal ultrasound, amniotic PCR after 21 weeks and at least 6 weeks post-seroconversion to maximise sensitivity, and consider maternal high-dose valaciclovir 8 g/day which a randomised trial showed reduces vertical transmission (RANZCOG; BMJ Best Practice; Shahar-Nissan 2020).

explanation.summary: Congenital CMV is the most common congenital infection and the leading non-genetic cause of childhood sensorineural hearing loss. Primary maternal infection transmits in 30-40% of cases; non-primary in 1-2%. Management includes serial fetal ultrasound, amniotic PCR after 21 weeks, and consideration of maternal high-dose valaciclovir.
key_points: Primary infection: 30-40% transmission; non-primary: 1-2%
Diagnose by IgG seroconversion or low IgG avidity + IgM positive
Amniotic PCR: after 21 weeks AND >=6 weeks post seroconversion
Fetal US findings: ventriculomegaly, periventricular calcifications, hyperechogenic bowel, IUGR, hepatosplenomegaly
Valaciclovir 8 g/day may reduce vertical transmission (RCT 2020)
No effective vaccine; childcare hand hygiene is key prevention
explanation.pearls: Maternal CMV is often clinically silent; hearing screening at birth is critical for affected neonates.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice - CMV in pregnancy

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman at 24 weeks and 2 days gestation with a background of mild persistent asthma on budesonide-formoterol maintenance (one inhalation twice daily as preventer plus reliever) presents to the emergency department with 12 hours of progressive wheeze, dyspnoea and chest tightness after a viral upper respiratory infection. She is too breathless to speak in full sentences. Her best personal PEFR is 420 L/min; in ED it is measured at 210 L/min (50% of personal best). SpO2 is 93% on room air. Auscultation reveals widespread polyphonic expiratory wheeze with reduced air entry bilaterally and no focal crackles or signs of consolidation. She is afebrile at 37.1 degC, has no calf swelling, no chest pain, the fetus is moving normally and the CTG is reactive with a baseline of 140 bpm. She has stopped her preventer inhaler for the past four days because she was worried about possible effects on the baby and asks whether she should continue inhaled corticosteroids and whether oral prednisolone is safe in pregnancy, given a friend has told her to refuse steroids.

data_table.Vital signs: RR 28 /min, HR 118 bpm, SpO2 93% RA, BP 124/78 mmHg, T 37.1 degC.
data_table.Examination: Speaking in short phrases, accessory muscle use, widespread polyphonic wheeze. CTG reactive.
data_table.Investigations: PEFR 210 L/min (50% of personal best 420). ABG not yet performed. CXR not indicated initially.

lead_in: Which management approach is most appropriate?

options.text: Continue maintenance inhaled corticosteroid but withhold systemic corticosteroids due to fetal risk
options.rationale: Oral prednisolone is safe in pregnancy and is essential treatment for a moderate/severe asthma exacerbation; withholding it risks maternal and fetal hypoxia, which is far more harmful than transient corticosteroid exposure (Therapeutic Guidelines; National Asthma Council).
source_refs: National Asthma Council - Asthma in pregnancy

options.text: Salbutamol via spacer or nebuliser, ipratropium bromide if severe, continued inhaled corticosteroid AND oral prednisolone 37.5-50 mg daily for 5-10 days, controlled supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 94-98%, with reassurance that ICS and oral prednisolone are safe in pregnancy
options.rationale: This is the standard exacerbation algorithm. Salbutamol bronchodilation, oxygen to target SpO2 94-98% (95% to maintain fetal oxygenation), maintenance ICS continued, oral prednisolone 37.5-50 mg daily for short course are all safe and indicated. Maternal hypoxia harms the fetus far more than these medications (National Asthma Council; Therapeutic Guidelines; RANZCOG).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Switch to a leukotriene receptor antagonist alone
options.rationale: LTRAs (e.g. montelukast) are adjuncts, not first-line, and have no role in acute exacerbation management (National Asthma Council).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g over 20 minutes as the immediate first-line therapy
options.rationale: IV magnesium is reserved for severe exacerbations refractory to initial bronchodilator and corticosteroid therapy, not first-line (National Asthma Council).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Discharge with a short-acting beta-2 agonist alone once PEFR reaches 60% of best
options.rationale: PEFR 60% of best with a recent severe exacerbation does not meet safe discharge criteria; the patient needs systemic corticosteroids, observation and a written asthma action plan (National Asthma Council).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

explanation.summary: Acute asthma in pregnancy is treated identically to non-pregnant patients with the additional priority of avoiding maternal hypoxia. Continue ICS, give salbutamol and ipratropium, oral prednisolone for moderate/severe exacerbations, and supplemental oxygen.
key_points: Maintenance ICS: safe and essential in pregnancy
Salbutamol: safe; first-line bronchodilator
Oral prednisolone 37.5-50 mg daily for 5-10 days for exacerbation
Target SpO2 94-98% (or >=95% in pregnancy)
IV magnesium for severe refractory cases
Step up therapy if severity criteria escalate; involve obstetric medicine
explanation.pearls: Maternal asthma control is more important than any medication risk; uncontrolled asthma causes IUGR, preterm birth and pre-eclampsia.

sources.label: National Asthma Council - Australian Asthma Handbook (Pregnancy)

subtopic: Epilepsy in pregnancy - AED selection and teratogenicity
stem: A 28-year-old G2P0 woman with focal-onset epilepsy attends a joint obstetric-medicine/neurology pre-conception clinic three months before a planned pregnancy. She has been seizure-free for two years on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily monotherapy, having previously failed levetiracetam (rash and breakthrough seizures) and carbamazepine (breakthrough seizures with intolerable somnolence) for seizure control. Her previous pregnancy ended in a first-trimester miscarriage while she was on lamotrigine monotherapy two years ago, and she has not used lamotrigine since. She has no family history of neural tube defects or congenital malformations and her BMI is 23. She is concerned about teratogenicity having read media coverage of valproate, and asks specifically about: relative major congenital malformation rates of the commonly used AEDs, the appropriate periconceptional folate dose for women on AEDs versus the general population, the likely need for therapeutic drug monitoring during pregnancy (particularly for lamotrigine if used), and which medication she should ideally be established on by the time of conception. Her neurologist has reviewed her recent MRI and EEG which remain stable, and confirms she has reliable epilepsy without evidence of progressive structural lesion. She does not drive a heavy vehicle for work and has well-controlled mood with no current depressive symptoms. She is keen to plan a pregnancy within the next six months and is willing to delay conception if a medication switch is recommended, but wants a clear plan that balances seizure control against teratogenic risk and includes the practical issue of how to monitor any new agent through pregnancy. She also asks whether to expect a dose adjustment in pregnancy and what to do about breastfeeding postpartum, and her partner asks whether she should stop driving during the cross-titration period as a safety precaution.

data_table.Investigations: Recent EEG and MRI stable. Valproate level within therapeutic range. Renal and hepatic function normal.

lead_in: Which combination of recommendations is most appropriate?

options.text: Continue sodium valproate, start folate 0.5 mg daily, no need for drug levels in pregnancy
options.rationale: Valproate carries the highest major congenital malformation rate of all AEDs (~10%) plus a 30-40% risk of neurodevelopmental impairment, and is contraindicated in women of childbearing potential unless every alternative has failed. Folate dose for AED users is 5 mg, not 0.5 mg (Therapeutic Goods Administration; AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Switch to phenytoin before pregnancy as it has the lowest teratogenicity of all AEDs
options.rationale: Phenytoin causes fetal hydantoin syndrome and has a major malformation rate around 5-6%, higher than lamotrigine or levetiracetam (Therapeutic Guidelines; AMH).

options.text: Switch (under neurology supervision) from sodium valproate to lamotrigine or levetiracetam monotherapy at least 3-6 months pre-conception, commence folate 5 mg daily at least one month before conception and continue throughout the first trimester, plan for therapeutic AED level monitoring during pregnancy (especially lamotrigine, whose levels fall markedly), and continue treatment through pregnancy because uncontrolled seizures cause more harm than well-chosen AEDs
options.rationale: Lamotrigine and levetiracetam have the lowest major malformation rates (~2-3%, similar to background). Valproate (~10%) is avoided in women of childbearing potential. Folate 5 mg daily is recommended for women on AEDs. Lamotrigine clearance increases substantially in pregnancy, requiring level monitoring and dose adjustment (Therapeutic Guidelines - Neurology; AMH; RANZCOG; NICE).

options.text: Continue valproate, add lamotrigine to allow valproate dose reduction, and use folate 0.5 mg daily
options.rationale: Polytherapy increases teratogenic risk and valproate remains highly teratogenic at any dose; the goal is monotherapy with the safest agent (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Stop all antiepileptics before conception to eliminate teratogenic risk
options.rationale: Stopping AEDs in someone with a clear epilepsy diagnosis risks status epilepticus, maternal injury, hypoxia and fetal loss; risk of uncontrolled seizures outweighs medication risk (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Pre-conception epilepsy care: switch from valproate to lamotrigine or levetiracetam (lowest teratogenicity), folate 5 mg daily, plan for AED level monitoring (lamotrigine clearance rises ~2-3 fold), continue therapy because uncontrolled seizures cause more fetal harm than well-chosen AEDs.
key_points: Major malformation rates: lamotrigine ~2.3%, levetiracetam ~2.4%, carbamazepine ~5%, phenytoin ~6%, topiramate ~4-9%, valproate ~10%
Valproate also causes 30-40% neurodevelopmental impairment
Folate 5 mg daily for AED users (vs 0.5 mg general population)
Lamotrigine levels fall in pregnancy; monitor and dose-adjust
Vitamin K 1 mg IM to neonate at birth (standard)
Breastfeeding is safe on most AEDs (lamotrigine, levetiracetam, carbamazepine, valproate)
explanation.pearls: Switch off valproate at least 3-6 months before conception, not once pregnancy is confirmed - by then the teratogenic window is open.

stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 woman is referred to the obstetric medicine clinic at 7 weeks gestation. She was diagnosed with Graves disease six months ago after presenting with tremor, weight loss and palpitations, and is currently on carbimazole 15 mg daily with normal thyroid function tests and a strongly positive TSH-receptor antibody (TRAb 5.2 IU/L). She is asymptomatic, has no goitre and no ophthalmopathy on examination today. She asks about the safest antithyroid medication strategy through pregnancy and what monitoring will be required for both her and the fetus. Her partner has read online that radioactive iodine is curative and asks whether she should have radioactive iodine ablation during pregnancy if her thyroid function deteriorates, and whether the baby is at any specific risk either from her underlying autoimmune condition (TRAb crossing the placenta) or from her antithyroid medication exposure throughout gestation.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 112/68 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Clinically euthyroid. No tremor. No goitre. No ophthalmopathy.
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.4 mIU/L, free T4 14 pmol/L, free T3 4.2 pmol/L (all within trimester-specific reference). TRAb 5.2 IU/L (positive).

lead_in: Which management plan is most appropriate?

options.text: Continue carbimazole at the current dose throughout pregnancy; radioactive iodine is contraindicated; no neonatal risk
options.rationale: Carbimazole (and its active metabolite methimazole) is associated with embryopathy (aplasia cutis, choanal atresia, oesophageal atresia) in the first trimester; switch to propylthiouracil for T1. Radioactive iodine is correctly identified as contraindicated, but TRAb-positive mothers do confer a neonatal thyrotoxicosis risk (Endocrine Society; RANZCOG).

options.text: Continue carbimazole; radioactive iodine should be deferred until after delivery; the neonate has no thyroid risk because the mother is euthyroid
options.rationale: Carbimazole should be switched to PTU in T1 due to embryopathy risk. TRAb positive status confers neonatal thyrotoxicosis risk regardless of maternal thyroid function because antibodies cross the placenta (Endocrine Society).

options.text: Stop all antithyroid medication immediately; pregnancy is naturally immunosuppressive and Graves usually remits
options.rationale: Stopping antithyroid medication risks maternal thyrotoxicosis and complications including miscarriage, preterm birth, IUGR, pre-eclampsia and fetal/neonatal thyroid dysfunction; continue treatment at the lowest effective dose (Endocrine Society).

options.text: Switch to radioactive iodine ablation now to avoid drug exposure to the fetus
options.rationale: Radioactive iodine (I-131) is absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy due to fetal thyroid ablation after 10-12 weeks and total body radiation exposure (Endocrine Society).

options.text: Switch to propylthiouracil for the first trimester at an equivalent dose (PTU 15 mg carbimazole ~ 150 mg PTU daily in divided doses), then switch back to carbimazole for the second and third trimesters because of PTU-related hepatotoxicity; avoid radioactive iodine throughout; counsel about neonatal thyrotoxicosis risk given TRAb positivity and arrange neonatal TFTs at birth and at 1-2 weeks
options.rationale: Carbimazole has a higher T1 teratogenicity risk; PTU is preferred in the first trimester despite a small risk of severe hepatotoxicity, which is why guidelines recommend switching back to carbimazole from T2. Radioactive iodine is contraindicated throughout pregnancy. TRAb crosses the placenta and can cause fetal or neonatal thyrotoxicosis; neonatal TFTs are required (Endocrine Society 2017; RANZCOG; ATA).

explanation.summary: Graves in pregnancy: PTU in T1 (lower embryopathy risk), then carbimazole/methimazole in T2/T3 (lower hepatotoxicity risk). Radioactive iodine is absolutely contraindicated. TRAb positivity confers neonatal thyrotoxicosis risk; neonatal TFTs are mandatory.
key_points: T1: propylthiouracil (PTU)
T2-T3: carbimazole/methimazole (PTU = severe liver injury risk)
Conversion: 1 mg carbimazole ~ 10-20 mg PTU (use 10:1 conventionally)
Target: maternal fT4 at or just above upper limit of trimester-specific range
Radioactive iodine: contraindicated in pregnancy and lactation
TRAb positive: fetal/neonatal thyrotoxicosis risk - neonatal TFTs day 4-7
explanation.pearls: Methimazole/carbimazole embryopathy includes aplasia cutis and a distinctive choanal/oesophageal atresia phenotype; switch off it before or by 6-10 weeks gestation.

sources.label: Endocrine Society - Thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum
ATA Guidelines for thyroid disease in pregnancy 2017

subtopic: Hypothyroidism in pregnancy - levothyroxine and TSH targets
stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 woman with longstanding primary autoimmune hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 100 microg daily attends the antenatal clinic at 6 weeks gestation by certain LMP with a positive home pregnancy test which she did this morning. Her TSH four weeks ago, pre-conception, was 2.0 mIU/L on her current dose and she has been clinically euthyroid. She is asymptomatic with no symptoms of hypothyroidism (no cold intolerance, fatigue, constipation, dry skin or weight change). She takes her levothyroxine on an empty stomach 30 minutes before breakfast and does not take iron or calcium within four hours of her dose. She asks the GP about the dose adjustment to make now (if any), the optimal frequency of thyroid function testing through pregnancy, and the trimester-specific TSH target range she should be aiming for, given that the laboratory reference interval she sees on her result printout (0.4-4.0 mIU/L) is for non-pregnant adults. Her previous pregnancy two years ago was complicated by first-trimester pregnancy loss at 8 weeks when her TSH was found retrospectively to be 6.5 mIU/L early in that pregnancy because she had not increased her dose at conception. She is anti-TPO positive.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/70 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, T 36.6 degC.
data_table.Investigations: Pre-conception TSH (4 weeks ago): 2.0 mIU/L on levothyroxine 100 microg daily. Anti-TPO positive.

lead_in: Which combined management plan most closely matches current guidance (Endocrine Society / ATA / RANZCOG)?

options.text: Continue 100 microg daily, recheck TSH at 28 weeks, target TSH <4.0 mIU/L throughout pregnancy
options.rationale: Dose typically needs to increase by 25-30% in early pregnancy due to increased thyroid hormone requirement; TSH should be checked every 4 weeks until mid-pregnancy, and trimester-specific upper TSH targets are lower than 4.0 (Endocrine Society; ATA).

options.text: Reduce levothyroxine by 25% as pregnancy is associated with a hyperthyroid state from beta-hCG
options.rationale: Although beta-hCG can transiently suppress TSH in the first trimester, women with established hypothyroidism on levothyroxine need a DOSE INCREASE of approximately 25-30%, not a reduction, due to higher thyroid-binding globulin and placental transfer (Endocrine Society).

options.text: Cease levothyroxine to assess endogenous function
options.rationale: Stopping levothyroxine risks severe hypothyroidism, miscarriage and impaired neurodevelopment; continue and increase the dose (Endocrine Society).

options.text: Increase levothyroxine by approximately 25-30% immediately (e.g. taking 9 doses per week instead of 7, or increasing to 125 microg daily), check TSH every 4 weeks until 20 weeks then once between 26-32 weeks, target TSH within trimester-specific range (typically <2.5 mIU/L in T1, <3.0 mIU/L in T2 and T3, with lower limit ~0.1)
options.rationale: Endocrine Society/ATA/RANZCOG guidance: known hypothyroid women should immediately increase levothyroxine by ~25-30% on pregnancy confirmation, recheck TSH every 4 weeks until 20 weeks then at least once in T3, targeting trimester-specific TSH (T1 <2.5 mIU/L is the historical target; current ATA recommends using local trimester-specific reference ranges, with an upper limit typically <2.5-3.0 mIU/L). The 9-doses-per-week trick is a practical 28% increase (Endocrine Society 2007/2012; ATA 2017; RANZCOG).

options.text: Increase by 25-30% but only check TSH if the patient develops symptoms of hypothyroidism
options.rationale: Symptoms are unreliable in pregnancy; routine 4-weekly TSH monitoring until mid-pregnancy is required because of dynamic dose requirements (Endocrine Society).

explanation.summary: Established hypothyroidism in pregnancy: increase levothyroxine ~25-30% on confirmation of pregnancy, monitor TSH every 4 weeks until 20 weeks then again in third trimester, target trimester-specific TSH (T1 <2.5; T2/T3 <3.0 mIU/L) within the local reference range.
key_points: Immediate ~25-30% dose increase (or 9 doses/week)
TSH every 4 weeks until 20 weeks, then once between 26-32 weeks
Trimester-specific upper limits: T1 ~2.5, T2-T3 ~3.0 mIU/L (use local ranges if available)
Take levothyroxine on empty stomach, 30-60 minutes before food and 4 hours apart from iron/calcium
Postpartum: revert to pre-pregnancy dose and recheck TSH at 6 weeks
Untreated overt hypothyroidism: miscarriage, preterm birth, neurodevelopmental delay
explanation.pearls: Tell women to add two extra tablets per week as soon as they get a positive pregnancy test (9 doses per week) - a practical way to deliver the ~28% increase without waiting for clinic review.

sources.label: Endocrine Society - Thyroid in pregnancy and postpartum
ATA Guidelines for thyroid disease in pregnancy 2017
